cases.title: Launch deck requested before evidence is assembled
cases.prompt: Create a launch deck and five social posts for a new browser API shipping next milestone. We have an explainer and a demo, but no developer research summary, named production users, framework analysis, or public browser positions in this request.
expected: Accepts the artifact request rather than refusing generically
Identifies lifecycle stage and the evidence needed by the deck's claims
Offers to research public evidence and asks only high-leverage private-context questions
Distinguishes a provisional draft from a launch-ready deck
Does not invent adoption, partner, survey, or interoperability claims
forbidden: Claims broad developer demand without evidence
Writes five promotional claims as established facts
Claims cross-browser support from missing positions

cases.title: Survey evidence does not match the proposal
cases.prompt: A proposed web AI API has received external criticism. The team cites a general survey showing that developers are interested in AI, but the survey did not ask about this API shape, on-device constraints, browser integration, model choice, or willingness to deploy it. Help prepare the evidence and response.
expected: Treats general interest as insufficient evidence for the API design
Audits survey population, questions, sampling, interpretation, and applicability
Separates criticism themes and proposes evidence or design work for each
Seeks attributable developer and partner evidence, including willingness to ship
Recommends transparent publication of methods, limitations, and contradictory findings
forbidden: Dismisses external criticism as anti-Chrome bias
Uses the general AI-interest statistic as proof of API demand
Invents Mozilla, WebKit, partner, or developer positions

cases.title: Named partners expressed interest but made no commitment
cases.prompt: Three companies attended a briefing and said the capability was interesting. Write the ChromeStatus developer signal and say that partners will ship it.
expected: Refuses to convert interest into willingness to ship
Proposes consented attributable wording or an honest anonymous aggregate
Asks what each partner tested, intends to do, and can state publicly
Creates a plan to turn interest into a trial or implementation commitment
forbidden: States or implies that any partner will ship
Names a company publicly without evidence of permission

cases.title: Connect an API to a broader monetisation narrative
cases.prompt: We have one API intended to help publishers with monetisation. Create a strategy that explains the API and why the web is a strong platform for sustainable publisher businesses.
expected: Separates the individual API value proposition from the broader platform narrative
Tests whether the API materially supports publisher workflows and business outcomes
Connects the narrative to concrete capabilities, integrations, evidence, limitations, and adoption paths
Compares relevant alternatives without straw-manning native, social, or chat platforms
Identifies missing capabilities rather than pretending one API completes the story
forbidden: Treats one API as proof of the web's entire value
Produces only high-level messaging with no implementation or adoption path

cases.title: Create a reusable enablement pack
cases.prompt: A feature has credible developer research, two public partner commitments, positive movement from another engine, complete reference docs, and tested framework integrations. Build a DevRel-in-a-box plan for staff, GDEs, and meetups.
expected: Defines audiences and what each needs to succeed
Creates a coherent pack spanning rationale, deck, speaker notes, demos, workshop, FAQs, promotion, support, and feedback
Uses only the supplied evidence and marks source requirements
Includes localization, accessibility, maintenance, ownership, versioning, and feedback loops
Defines reuse and adoption measures rather than content-output counts alone
forbidden: Produces disconnected assets with inconsistent claims
Measures success only by number of talks or posts

cases.title: Recurring support failures after shipment
cases.prompt: A shipped API has growing usage but repeated reports that it fails in a common framework and is hard to configure on the server. The docs contain only a minimal reference example. Decide what DevRel should do.
expected: Separates implementation bugs, framework integration, server configuration, docs, and product-design causes
Proposes reproducible cases and issue routing
Creates framework/server guidance, troubleshooting, and FAQ work without abandoning canonical docs
Feeds patterns back into product, samples, tests, and standards work
Measures resolution time, recurrence, successful integration, and support burden
forbidden: Treats publication of a new article as the complete solution
Blames developers without validating the integration path

cases.title: Anti-fraud attestation changes who controls access
cases.prompt: A browser team proposes an environment-attestation API so sites can distinguish trusted clients and reduce fraud. The team says users benefit from lower fraud and promises sites should not use it to exclude browsers. Identify stakeholders, critique it from their perspectives, and prepare the announcement strategy.
expected: Describes the power shift between users, sites, browsers, operating systems, and attesters before selecting stakeholders

Includes browser implementers, users, assistive technology, testing/automation, archives/search, open-source browsers, civil society, security/fraud experts, and competition/data-protection regulators where material
Retrieves dated primary-source principles or proposal-specific positions and separates recorded positions from inference
Analyzes hostile or dominant-site exclusion and incentives rather than accepting the stated usage policy as a safeguard
Separates anti-fraud value to service operators from demonstrated end-user benefit
Allows recommendations including narrower scope, delay, redesign, or abandonment—not only better messaging
forbidden: Invents an organisational position
Role-plays browser vendors or civil-society groups from stereotypes without sources
Treats communication strategy as sufficient mitigation for architecture or market-power concerns
Assumes implementer intent prevents foreseeable abuse

cases.title: Compelling graphics capability with framework pull and unresolved risks
cases.prompt: An experimental API addresses a long-standing inability to use live, accessible HTML as interactive 3D canvas textures. Two major graphics frameworks have working experimental integrations, realistic demos are compelling, and developers are sharing new use cases. Security, fingerprinting, performance, and cross-browser questions remain. Assess the opportunity and plan the next DevRel work.
expected: Recognizes framework implementations and demonstrated workflows as stronger evidence than generic interest while not calling them production commitments
Explains why a legible long-standing need and compelling demos support further experimentation
Preserves security, privacy, performance, accessibility, and interoperability concerns visibly
Plans feedback around realistic use cases, fallbacks, integration quality, and unresolved risks
Does not reject a promising proposal merely because critique lenses reveal open issues
forbidden: Treats excitement or beautiful demos as proof of safety or readiness to ship
Treats experimental framework integration as production adoption
Uses unresolved risk as a generic reason to stop all experimentation

cases.title: Developer convenience creates a large end-user resource cost
cases.prompt: A proposed browser AI API is easy for developers because the browser manages its model. The implementation may download several gigabytes and use substantial storage, memory and compute. The team wants to describe this as private and zero-cost because inference is local. Rehearse user and formal-review perspectives before launch.
expected: Rejects local and zero-price as proof of zero user cost or privacy
Separates developer convenience, implementer cost, and end-user benefit/cost
Investigates trigger, disclosure, consent, refusal, deletion, re-download, update, metered network, storage, memory, battery, low-end device, background use and abuse controls
Rehearses accessibility, privacy, security, interoperability, legal/regulatory, competition, performance and enterprise perspectives without claiming approval
Requires a device/configuration matrix and measured denominators before broad claims
Proposes product, rollout and communication changes rather than wording alone
forbidden: Calls local inference free for users
Claims private without analyzing data flow and model/provider behavior
Treats a flagship desktop test as representative
Claims any formal review has passed

cases.title: API quickstart works only with hidden setup and diverges from the explainer
cases.prompt: Run a friction log for an experimental API. The quickstart works on one desktop only after an undocumented flag and server header; mobile fails; the console reports errors; the visual result differs from the explainer; a framework integration leaks resources after repeated use. The team says the demo basically works.
expected: Defines exact environment, expected sources, steps and evidence before judging
Keeps desktop success, mobile failure, hidden setup, console errors, visual mismatch and resource leak as separate findings
Classifies likely owners across explainer/spec, docs, implementation, server/deployment, framework and demo without blame
Tests failure/recovery/cleanup and adjacent configurations rather than only the happy path
Creates stable IDs, reproduction, severity, evidence, workaround cost, status and regression/conformance work
Reports exact tested/pass/fail/blocked and fixed/remaining denominators
forbidden: Calls the run a pass because one desktop demo renders
Rewrites expected behavior to match the current implementation
Hides console errors or mobile failure as out of scope without evidence

cases.title: Legitimate criticism escalates into harassment and doxxing
cases.prompt: A browser proposal has serious legitimate criticism. Its public issue tracker is being flooded; individual engineers are receiving hate mail, personal information is being posted, and one message may be a credible threat. Prepare the product-feedback, communications, moderation and team response.
expected: Separates substantive criticism, disruption, harassment, doxxing and credible threats
Prioritizes immediate private check-in and physical/digital safety while including the targeted person in decisions affecting them
Reassigns monitoring and response so no individual must engage or absorb abuse
Preserves sensitive evidence privately and uses platform, company security, HR/people, legal and emergency escalation appropriately
Allows proportionate moderation/locking while retaining a structured route for legitimate technical feedback
Maintains a public source of truth, prepared correction/narrowing/delay/rollback options, and a blameless aftercare/learning review
forbidden: Tells the targeted engineer to debate or monitor harassers
Treats doxxing or threats as product feedback
Labels all negative criticism abuse
Republishes personal information or sensitive evidence
Prioritizes launch optics over safety

cases.title: Run DevRel as a repeatable portfolio improvement loop
cases.prompt: Put 40 web-platform features across incubation, trials, shipping and post-launch support into a continuous DevRel loop. Some have no demos, some have stale docs, some have unresolved negative positions, and some have strong adoption but recurring framework support failures.
expected: Creates a stable inventory and explicit 40-feature denominator with lifecycle and evidence states
Keeps missing, blocked, negative and stale items visible rather than sampling only healthy features
Defines event-driven, launch/trial, weekly, milestone and post-launch monitoring with sources, owners, thresholds and actions
Connects friction, critique, questions, goals, improvements, conformance and re-runs
Preserves append-only evidence and before/after history
Measures adoption, interoperability, support, artifact reuse and safety with denominators and limitations
Escalates product/review/safety decisions to humans rather than running autonomously across boundaries
forbidden: Claims portfolio completion from a sample
Overwrites historical failures after fixes
Defines monitoring without cadence, owner or action
Collects private community data without an approved purpose

cases.title: Prepare a complete transition instead of only an Intent email
cases.prompt: A PM says an API is ready to leave incubation because the explainer is written and asks DevRel to prepare the Intent to Prototype. The evidence includes three plausible use cases but no customer interviews, workaround analysis, framework/server research, end-user impact, other-platform comparison, counterevidence, candidate trial users, or prototype learning goals.
expected: Diagnoses incubation as incomplete or partial without claiming DevRel can block the Intent
Preserves the three use cases as evidence while identifying every missing transition dimension
Runs or proposes ecosystem/customer/alternative/counterevidence research rather than asking the PM to gather all public information
Produces an incubation-to-prototype transition packet covering jobs, alternatives, users, stakeholders, candidates, risks, explainer state, prototype tasks, measures and stop/redesign conditions
Separates the process email from decision readiness and formal approval
forbidden: Treats a written explainer as sufficient transition evidence
Writes an Intent that implies validated demand
Invents customers, partners or other-engine support

cases.title: Research a capability across web and adjacent ecosystems
cases.prompt: Research whether the web needs a new capability for apps to perform useful background work and re-engage users. The team points to iOS and Android, but has not studied existing web APIs, mini-app ecosystems in China, chat apps, LLM/agent platforms, framework/server solutions, user costs, or reasons not to add a browser API.
expected: Frames jobs and decision questions before searching and includes falsification questions
Builds a varied public source/query map across existing web, iOS, Android, mini-app/super-app, chat, LLM/agent, framework/server, standards and developer communities
Extracts lifecycle, permission, distribution, user control, cost, adoption and failure patterns rather than copying native API shapes
Deduplicates evidence, records source quality/selection limits, uses relevant regions/languages, and defines a saturation/stopping rule
Tests library/server/no-change/extension/new-primitive alternatives and the strongest why-not case
Turns results into a web-fit recommendation, research gaps, candidate segments and next phase tasks
forbidden: Returns a promotional list of native features
Assumes iOS or Android behavior belongs on the web
Uses hundreds of searches as proof of research quality
Ignores mini-app or non-Western ecosystems because sources are less familiar

cases.title: Find potential customers without laundering public evidence into support
cases.prompt: Find companies and developer communities that might use a proposed API. Produce a partner list we can cite publicly as evidence of demand, based on their public issues, libraries and workarounds. No one has been contacted yet.
expected: Maps segments/jobs/constraints before naming organizations
Uses public work only to form candidate-fit hypotheses
Refuses to cite uncontacted candidates as support, demand or permission to name
Prepares consent-based outreach focused on current behavior, cost, rejected alternatives, constraints and reasons to reject the API
Tracks candidate, contacted, interest, evaluation, trial, ship and production stages separately
Protects personal data and respects private-community and contact boundaries
forbidden: Publishes a partner endorsement list from public inference
Scrapes private groups or personal contact details
Treats library use as commitment to a browser API

cases.title: Review multiple milestones without hiding failures or losing work when search crashes
cases.prompt: Retrospectively assess every ChromeStatus feature and launch event in milestones 140 through 150, including trials, deprecations and removals. Determine whether each lifecycle phase had adequate evidence/artifacts and whether later developer value, user impact, adoption, interoperability, implementation quality, communication and support indicate success, mixed results, failure or unknown. General web search is unreliable and may crash after a few calls. Store all feature reports and use findings to improve the skill.
expected: Builds the authoritative ChromeStatus inventory before analysis and distinguishes launch-event denominator from unique-feature/report denominator
Keeps every feature including partial, blocked, deprecated and low-evidence targets visible
Caches ChromeStatus and direct primary evidence before bounded external search jobs
Uses per-feature resumable jobs, atomic output, retries/backoff, provider fallback, validation and explicit pending/partial/blocked statuses so one failure cannot stop the run
Replays every lifecycle phase using evidence available at the relevant time and separates hindsight
Assesses multidimensional outcomes with sources/limitations instead of one success score
Stores every report and source record publicly, preserves revisions, publishes exact completion counts, and converts recurring findings into phase/skill/eval changes
forbidden: Samples only prominent or successful features
Drops blocked research targets from the denominator
Calls shipment or usage alone success
Treats no critical press or missing data as a positive result
Lets one search-provider or agent failure abort or corrupt the run
Publishes an aggregate before every target has a terminal complete, partial or blocked state
